title: Observable measures of multipartite entanglement
abstract: Multipartite entanglement is the premier resource for quantum technologies. Yet, its exact quantification in the laboratory is notoriously challenging, typically requiring the full knowledge of high dimensional quantum states. Here, we construct observable bounds to multipartite entanglement for systems of arbitrary size, which are functions of the local and global state purities, and correlation functions. First, we derive experimentally accessible upper and lower limits to both the bipartite entanglement of formation and the squashed entanglement of bipartite systems, by leveraging cornerstone results of quantum information theory: the entropy strong subadditivity inequality and the Koashi-Winter monogamy relation. Then, we convert them into bounds to the entanglement up to degree k for arbitrary states, and to the genuine k-partite entanglement, by employing a recently proposed method. Finally, we analytically and numerically test these results, by bounding the multipartite entanglement of several relevant states and mixtures, including the important classes of GHZ, Dicke, W states, and random pure states.
summary: This paper presents a new method to estimate multipartite entanglement using only observable quantities like state purities and correlations. It avoids the exponential cost of full state tomography and provides a scalable way to certify entanglement in large-scale quantum systems. The approach is particularly useful for characterizing GHZ, W, and Dicke states in the presence of noise.
main_problem: Quantifying multipartite entanglement in laboratory settings is extremely difficult because it typically requires full state tomography or computationally intractable calculations for mixed states.
main_result: The authors developed a framework of experimentally accessible upper and lower bounds for multipartine entanglement (including entanglement of formation and squashed entanglement) using only local and global state purities and correlation functions.
method: The approach uses the entropy strong subadditivity inequality and the Koashi-Winter monogamy relation to derive bounds, and employs a dynamic programming strategy to efficiently compute these bounds for large systems.
model_or_system: The framework applies to arbitrary-sized multipartite quantum systems, with specific analytical and numerical tests performed on GHZ, W, and Dicke states, as well as random pure states.
key_observables: Local and global state purities, correlation functions of local observables, and operator norms.
important_parameters: System dimension (d), number of particles (N), entanglement degree (k), and noise/purity parameters (p).
main_assumptions: The bounds are most effective when the global state is nearly pure; the method assumes that higher-order purities are measurable, though higher-order expansions increase measurement complexity.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows how bounds certify N-partite entanglement in dephased GHZ states; Figure 2 illustrates the scaling of entanglement bounds for GHZ, W, and Dicke states; Figure 3 shows the entanglement structure of random pure states.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of entanglement quantification, derives bipartite bounds using information-theoretic inequalities, extends these to multipartite entanglement using a recursive method, and validates the results through analytical tests on symmetric states and numerical simulations of random states.
why_interesting: This is highly relevant for experimentalists in AMO and quantum optics as it provides a way to certify complex multipartite entanglement using low-depth measurements (purity and correlations) without the need for full state tomography.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 2. We study the scaling with the particle number N of the lower bound to the genuine N-partite entanglement of formation in symmetric pure states. While, as expected, the GHZ state has always one bit of N-partite entanglement, the Dicke states display peak entanglement when N is twice the number of excitations. We conjecture this behaviour is universal, i.e., it may hold for any N.

FIG. 1. The yellow line represents the total entanglement of formation E2↔N F (ρ(p)) in Eq. (24). The blue line shows that the lower bound of the total entanglement of formation increases as p varies from 0.8 to 1, where the state becomes an ideal N-partite GHZ state. The red line is the upper bound of E2↔N−1 F (ρ(p)), and it is always zero, matching the analytical value.

FIG. 3. We investigate the entanglement structure of ran- dom five-qubit pure states, as constructed in (34). In this case, the parameter of the Bernoulli distribution (33) is set to p = 0.85. For this choice, we calculate the (averaged) lower bounds to the k-partite entanglement of formation and squashed entanglement, for k = 2, 3, 4, 5.
